teamObjective.explanation: Partner (player 2) has only 2 cards remaining, so the immediate team objective is to give them a chance to win the trick or at least avoid seizing control with a weak hand. Leading the lowest card minimizes the chance I win the lead and lets the partner potentially take over.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner is near finishing their hand with only 2 cards left; their exact cards are unknown, but they likely retain some winning strength or a quick exit card.
evidence: handCounts shows partner (player 2) has 2 cards remaining
No public plays have occurred, so partner's card distribution is unobserved

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents have not played any cards, so their holdings are unknown. They may hold higher non-trump cards or trump cards that can beat a low diamond lead.
evidence: Opponents hold 3 and 3 cards respectively, more than partner

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of diamonds is the smallest legal play. It preserves higher cards for later, avoids winning a trick my weak hand cannot control, and gives the partner with 2 cards the best chance to take the lead or finish.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Slightly higher than the 3 but otherwise identical strategic value; keeping the 4 is marginally better if future plays require a higher low card.
Higher still and more likely to win against opponents holding 3s/4s, which would force me to retain lead with a very weak remaining hand.
The highest low-card option; it is more likely to take the trick and leave me leading with even weaker follow-up cards, undermining the goal of transferring lead to the partner.

risks: The 3 of diamonds may still win the trick if all other players pass or play lower, leaving me stuck leading with weaker follow-ups.
An opponent may play a trump or higher non-trump, winning control and preventing partner from finishing.
Partner may be unable to win the trick regardless of my lead.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By playing the lowest legal card, I minimize the probability that I win the trick myself and maximize the chance that control flows to the partner or a stronger position later.

notes: Opening lead with no table information; conservative low-card lead aligns with the partner-near-finish scenario.
